coredata.dc:title: Aggregation-induced emission carbon dots as Al3+-mediated nanoaggregate probe for rapid and selective detection of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hun Seo, Young
Elizabeth Aguilar Estrada, Diana
Jang, Dohyub
Baik, Seungyun
Lee, Jaeho
Ha Kim, Dong
Kim, Sehoon
coredata.dc:description: Worldwide abuse of tetracycline (TC) seriously threatens environmental safety and human health. Metal-TC complexes formed by residual TC in the environment can also contribute to the spread of antibiotic resistance. Therefore, monitoring of TC residues is still required. Here, we report novel aggregation-induced emission carbon dots (AIE-Cdots) as nanoaggregate probes for the rapid and selective detection of TC residue. Riboflavin precursors with rotational functional groups led to the development of AIE-Cdots. The aggregation of AIE-Cdots was induced selectively for Al3+, amplifying the fluorescence signals owing to the restricted rotation of the side chains on the AIE-Cdot surface. The fluorescence signal of such Al3+-mediated nanoaggregates (Al3+-NAs) was further triggered by the structural fixation of TC at the Al3+ active sites, suggesting the formation of TC-coordinated Al3+-NAs. A linear correlation was observed in the TC concentration range of 0–10 μM with a detection limit of 42 nM. In addition, the strong Al3+ binding affinity of AIE-Cdots produced similar NAs and enhanced fluorescence signals in Al3+–TC mixtures. These AIE-Cdots-based nanoplatforms have a rapid response, good selectivity, and reliable accuracy for detecting TC or aluminum complexes, meeting the requirements for hazardous substance monitoring and removal in environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation induced emission
Rotational functional group
Nanoaggregates
Al3+ ion
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of TC in milk samples
Preparation and structural characterization of AIE-Cdots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by research grants from the National Research Foundation of Korea (2019K1A4A7A02102530, and 2021R1A2C2005418) and the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) intramural program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of AIE-Cdots. (b) TEM and (c) HR-TEM images of AIE-Cdots in aqueous media. (d) XRD spectrum of AIE-Cdots. (e) FT-IR spectra of riboflavin (dark yellow line) and AIE-Cdots (cyan line). (f) Wide full scan XPS spectrum of AIE-Cdots. High-resolution XPS (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s, and (i) O 1 s spectra of AIE-Cdots.

(a) Schematic illustration of the energy diagram and aggregation behavior of AIE-Cdots in aprotic solvent environments. (b) UV–vis absorption and (c) fluorescence spectra of AIE-Cdots (excited at 390 nm) as a function of acetonitrile (ACN) volume fraction in ACN/H2O mixtures. The images are the corresponding fluorescence signal of AIE-Cdots from different environments under UV light at 365 nm. (d) TEM image of AIE-Cdots in an aprotic solvent environment (ACN/H2O = 99:1 v/v).

(a) Fluorescence spectral changes of AIE-Cdots upon the addition of Al3+ and different metal ions (50 μM) for 2 min in HEPES buffer (pH = 5.5). (b) TEM image (scale bar: 50 nm) and (c) EDX mapping images (scale bar: 1 μm) of Al3+-NAs. (d) Surface charge and (e) FT-IR spectra of AIE-Cdots (cyan color) and Al3+-NAs (royal color). (f) Possible aggregation mechanism of AIE-Cdots induced by Al3+.

(a) Temporal fluorescence profiles and spectral changes (Inset) of AIE-Cdots after the addition of Al3+ (100 μM). (b) Selective fluorescence response of AIE-Cdots toward 50 μM Al3+ in the presence of various metal ions (Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Cs+) at a concentration of 50 μM. (c) Fluorescence response and (d) intensity correlation of AIE-Cdots with different concentrations of Al3+ (0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μM).

(a) Schematic illustration of Al3+-NAs for TC detection. (b) Fluorescence spectra and (c) intensity profiles of Al3+-NAs upon the addition of 10 μM TC in the presence of various antibiotics at a concentration of 10 μM: (1) Al3+-NAs only; (2) TC; (3) TC + KAN; (4) TC + CIP; (5) TC + ERY; (6) TC + PEN; (7) TC + L. (d) Fluorescence response and intensity correlation (Inset) of Al3+-NAs with different concentrations of TC (0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μM). (e) Temporal fluorescence profiles and spectral changes (Inset) of Al3+-NAs after the addition of TC (10 μM). (f) TEM image, (g) HR-TEM image, and (h) EDX mapping images of TC-Al3+-NAs (scale bar: 1 μm).

(a) Schematic illustration of TC-Al3+-NAs prepared using different mixing processes. (b) Fluorescence spectral changes of AIE-Cdots, AIE-Cdots including TC, and each batch in aqueous media. (c) TEM image, and (d) HR-TEM image of Batch 2.

head.ce:title: Aggregation-induced emission carbon dots as Al3+-mediated nanoaggregate probe for rapid and selective detection of tetracycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These co-authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Worldwide abuse of tetracycline (TC) seriously threatens environmental safety and human health. Metal-TC complexes formed by residual TC in the environment can also contribute to the spread of antibiotic resistance. Therefore, monitoring of TC residues is still required. Here, we report novel aggregation-induced emission carbon dots (AIE-Cdots) as nanoaggregate probes for the rapid and selective detection of TC residue. Riboflavin precursors with rotational functional groups led to the development of AIE-Cdots. The aggregation of AIE-Cdots was induced selectively for Al3+, amplifying the fluorescence signals owing to the restricted rotation of the side chains on the AIE-Cdot surface. The fluorescence signal of such Al3+-mediated nanoaggregates (Al3+-NAs) was further triggered by the structural fixation of TC at the Al3+ active sites, suggesting the formation of TC-coordinated Al3+-NAs. A linear correlation was observed in the TC concentration range of 0–10 μM with a detection limit of 42 nM. In addition, the strong Al3+ binding affinity of AIE-Cdots produced similar NAs and enhanced fluorescence signals in Al3+–TC mixtures. These AIE-Cdots-based nanoplatforms have a rapid response, good selectivity, and reliable accuracy for detecting TC or aluminum complexes, meeting the requirements for hazardous substance monitoring and removal in environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the second-produced and consumed antibiotics worldwide, tetracycline (TC) has been widely used for disease prevention and growth promotion in animals because of its high antibacterial activity, minimal side effects, and low production cost [1–5]. However, excessive use of TC in livestock husbandry and agriculture continuously releases TC residues into the soil, wastewater, and potable water. These residues can enter the human body through the food chain and cause serious side effects, such as bacterial antibiotic resistance, hepatotoxicity, gastrointestinal disorders, and allergic reactions [6–8]. In addition to the effects of TC residues, several studies have highlighted the need for awareness of environmental and health threats posed by metal–TC complexes [9–11]. The strong coordination ability of TC for metal ions facilitates the formation of metal–TC complexes in natural environments [12]. The potential adverse effects of metal–TC complexes on biological systems by their toxicity, environmental degradation, and interaction with other pollutants are not clearly understood. However, metalloantibiotics are expanding their scope of use as antibacterial materials as well as potential alternatives to conventional antibiotics, increasing the attraction and demand for metal complexes [13,14]. Therefore, nanoplatforms capable of sensitive detection and effective removal of metal–TC complexes as well as residual TC in the environment should be urgently developed.

To date, various strategies for sensitive TC detection, including electrochemical [15], electrochemiluminescence [16,17], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [18], and enzyme-linked immunoassay [19], have been proposed. TC detection has also been performed using traditional analytical methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography (LC-MS) [20–22]. Among these methods, fluorescence analysis is considered an ideal analytical technique for TC detection because of its high sensitivity, fast response, and ease of operation [23–26]. The potential interest in valid methods based on fluorescence strategies has led to advances in functional nanomaterials. Metal nanosensors, such as gold nanostructures [27,28], metal–organic frameworks [29–32], and inorganic quantum dots [33–35], have been developed for TC monitoring, which is based on a well-known observation that TC forms complexes with a broad range of metal ions through strong interactions. However, metal nanosensors prepared using the above strategy are only applicable to TC detection and limited to detecting potential pollutants such as metal–TC complexes.

Carbon dots (Cdots) are emerging as attractive photonic nanoprobes for food detection and clinical diagnosis owing to their excellent biocompatibility, unique photophysical properties, and environmentally friendly characteristics [36–38]. Most reported Cdots exhibit an aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon similar to a typical organic dye because of increased non-radiative transition rate by π–π stacking interactions in the aggregated state. This leads to low sensitivity to fluorescence analysis as the nanosensors operate in the turn-off mode for certain analytes [39–41]. To address these drawbacks, aggregation-induced emission (AIE)-Cdots with enhanced fluorescence signals in the aggregated state have recently been designed [42–44]. Various surface capping ligands prohibit the surficial intramolecular rotation of Cdots when the Cdots are closely packed, amplifying the fluorescence signal due to the dominant radiative recombination rate. This has provided satisfactory outputs in various applications, including energy conversion and storage [45], soft robotics [46], light-emitting diodes [47], and biosensing [48–50]. Consequently, exploring efficient synthesis procedures and appropriate precursors to develop AIE-Cdots is essential.

Herein, we prepare novel AIE-Cdots by a one-step hydrothermal reaction using riboflavin and L-arginine as precursors. The blue fluorescence of AIE-Cdots was enhanced at similar wavelengths by self-aggregation. A comparative analysis with nonAIE-Cdots prepared from lumichrome (riboflavin derivative) without ribityl group further revealed that the rotational functional group of precursor gives AIE properties to the Cdots. The AIE-Cdots were formed into nanoaggregates through a highly selective binding reaction toward Al3+ and then used for monitoring TC residues. The fluorescence of Al3+-mediated nanoaggregates (Al3+-NAs) was enhanced by the restriction of the surface functional group rotation of AIE-Cdots, and Al3+ chelated to nanoaggregates acted as a specific binding site for recognizing TC residues. In the presence of TC, Al3+-NAs retained similar nanoaggregates without separating the Al3+–TC complex, while the fluorescence signal was further amplified by conformational fixation of TC coordinated in the nanoaggregates. Notably, the nanoaggregates induced by Al3+ were kept stable by binding to other reactive analytes, such as TC, which can be used not only for TC detection but also for TC removal. Consequently, sensitive monitoring with high selectivity for TC residues was successfully achieved. The practical application of nanoaggregates was verified through the sensitive detection of TC in synthetic urine and milk samples. Moreover, AIE-Cdots formed nanoaggregates with similar fluorescence signal enhancement for the Al3+–TC complexes, proving the availability as nanosensors for detecting aluminum complexes that could be considered potential pollutants.

Riboflavin (98.0 %) was supplied from Alfa Aesar (USA). Lumichrome, L-arginine (>99.5 %), aluminum chloride (AlCl3, 99.9 %), manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O, 99.0 %), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, >99.0 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99.9 %), iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O, 98.0 %), cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 98 %), copper(II) chloride (CuCl2, >99.0 %), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, >99.9 %), mercury(II) chloride (HgCl2, >99.5 %), cesium chloride (CsCl, 99.9 %), tetracycline (TC, >98.0 %), kanamycin sulfate (KAN, >99.0 %), ciprofloxacin (CIP, 98 %), erythromycin (ERY, >99.9 %), penicillin G sodium salt (PEN, >96.0 %), lincomycin hydrochloride (L, 99 %) and HEPES buffer solution (1 M, pH 5.5) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Phosphate buffer solutions (0.1 M) with a pH range of 6 to 8 were prepared using different proportions of KH2PO4 and K2HPO4. The desired pH (5, 5.5, 9) of a phosphate buffer solution was adjusted by adding 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using a Milli-Q Direct 8 water treatment system (Milli-Q, Merck KgaA, Germany).

The absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 35 UV–visible spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Walthan, MA, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured on an FL6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) with a 10 mm quartz emission cuvette from Helma. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a SHIMADZU IRSpirit with QATR-S. Zeta potential was determined using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK) at 25 °C. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 operated at 200 kV using carbon-film-coated 200 mesh copper grids. The chemical analyses of nanoaggregates were performed with a FEI Talos F200X operated a 200 kV and equipped with a Super-X energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE equipped with LynxEye. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Nexsa spectrometer.

The AIE-Cdots were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, riboflavin (90 mg) and L-arginine (416 mg) were mixed in 20 mL of ultrapure deionized (DI) water. The mixture was then placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Berghof BR-100, Eningen, Germany) for annealing at 200 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered using a 0.2 µm syringe filter to remove the solid precipitates. Then, the clear solution was purified in a dialysis membrane (0.1–0.5 kDa MWCO) with ultrapure DI water for 12 h. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried, and the obtained powder was stored at 4 °C for further use. nonAIE-Cdots were prepared using the same procedure but using lumichrome, a different precursor, instead of riboflavin.

AIE-Cdots (1 mg) were dispersed in 1 mL of DI water. Stock solutions (2 mM) of various metal ions (Al3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cs+, Cu2+, and Hg2+) were prepared in DI water. For the selectivity studies, 20 µL of AIE-Cdots and 100 µL of the prepared metal stock solution were added to 1.88 mL of HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.5) and incubated for 2 min at room temperature. For sensitivity studies of Al3+, 20 µL of AIE-Cdots and 100 µL of different concentrations of Al3+ (0–100 µM) were mixed with 1.88 mL of HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.5), and the mixture solution was incubated for 2 min at room temperature. The interference evaluation was performed by recording the fluorescence using 2 mL of the mixture solution. For this procedure, various metal ions (100 µM) were added to the HEPES buffer containing AIE-Cdots solution (20 µL) and Al3+ (100 µM). Fluorescence measurements were performed at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

Al3+-NAs solution was prepared by mixing 20 µL of AIE-Cdots (1 mg/mL) and 100 µL of Al3+ (2 mM) in 2 mL of HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.5). Stock solutions of 100 µM various antibiotics (TC, KAN, CIP, PEN, ERY, and L) and 2 mM metal ions (Co2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cs+, Cu2+, and Hg2+) were prepared in DI water. For the selectivity test, the Al3+-NAs solution (20 µL) and each prepared antibiotic stock solution (200 µL) were mixed with HEPES buffer (100 mM, pH 5.5) until complete a volume of 2 mL, and the solution was incubated for 2 min at room temperature. The sensitivity study was performed by recording the fluorescence of samples (2 mL) after the addition and 2 min incubation of 120 uL of Al3+-NAs to 1.88 mL of each solution containing different TC concentrations (0–10 µM) in HEPES buffer. For the interference test, the sample solutions were prepared by adding 100 µL of stock solutions of various antibiotics (10 µM) and metal ions (100 µM) to 1.90 mL of TC-Al3+-NAs solutions. For feasibility experiments, synthetic urine samples (non-biological diluent) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (MO, USA). The synthetic urine samples were diluted 100-fold with fresh water. The sensitivity towards TC was then examined after the addition of 120 uL of Al3+-NAs to 1.88 mL of diluted synthetic urine solution mixed with different concentrations of TC. Fluorescence measurements for all samples (final volume of 2 mL) were performed at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Commercial milk samples were purchased from a local market. The deproteinization process of milk samples was performed according to the literature [51]. First, trichloroacetic acid (1 %, v/v) was added to milk samples and sonicated for 10 min to precipitate proteins. The solutions were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min and then, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.20 μm membrane. The collected filtrate was diluted 100 times with fresh water for TC analysis. A standard calibration curve was established by spiking different concentrations of TC (0, 1, 2, 5, 8, 10 µM) into the deproteinized milk solution (1.88 mL) containing Al3+-NAs (120 µL). The final volume of each sample was set to 2 mL, and fluorescence measurements were performed at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

To study the detection potential of Al3+–TC complexes, three batches were prepared using different mixing procedures. Batch 1 was prepared by adding TC (100 µL, 100 µM) to the Al3+-NAs solution prepared by mixing Al3+ (100 µL, 2 mM) and AIE-Cdots (20 µL, 1 mg/mL). Batch 2 was obtained by adding AIE-Cdots (20 µL, 1 mg/mL) to a solution mixed with TC (100 µL, 100 µM) and Al3+ (100 µL, 2 mM). Batch 3 was prepared by mixing Al3+ (100 µL, 2 mM) with a solution containing AIE-Cdots (20 µL, 1 mg/mL) and TC (100 µL, 100 µM). The final volume was completed to 2 mL with fresh water and concentrations of the reagents included in all batches were 5 µM for TC, 100 µM for Al3+, and 0.02 mg for AIE-Cdots. The fluorescence spectra of all the batches were scanned after incubation for 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structural characterization of AIE-Cdots
ce:section.ce:para:
Inspired by previous studies showing that restricted rotation of the functional group on the Cdot surface provides AIE characteristics [44,46], we prepared AIE-Cdots via a hydrothermal reaction at a 1:10 M ratio by selecting riboflavin with ribityl side chain and L-arginine as precursors to attach rotatable functional groups to the AIE-Cdots surface ( Fig. 1
a). The TEM image of AIE-Cdots shows that they have a well-dispersed spherical shape with an average size of approximately 4.6 ± 1.19 nm (Fig. 1 b). In addition, a high-resolution image of AIE-Cdots shows a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 c), suggesting that AIE-Cdots are composed of (100) lattice planes similar to graphite carbon structures [52]. As displayed in Fig. 1 d, the XRD pattern of AIE-Cdots exhibits a broad diffraction peat at 23.5°, which is assigned to the carbon (002) plane.

The surface properties of AIE-Cdots were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy, and XPS analysis. The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 1 e shows the main functional groups of AIE-Cdots. The broad band at approximately 3100–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of hydroxyl (–OH) and amino (–NH2) groups. The peaks by stretching and bending vibrations of the methylene groups (–CH-/–CH2-) were observed at 2941 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1, respectively. The spectrum of AIE-Cdots also revealed the stretching vibrations for C = O/C = C and C–N at ∼ 1645–1582 and ∼ 1328 cm−1. The peak at 1376 cm−1 is due to the –OH bending vibration of alcoholic groups, and the ∼ 1073 cm−1 peak corresponds to the C–O stretching vibration of alcoholic groups. These results indicate the presence of an aromatic structure and various functional groups attached to the surface. As shown in Fig. 1 f, the XPS profile of Cdots shows that their elemental composition is 64.67 % C, 15.25 % N, and 20.08 % O. The high-resolution XPS spectrum provides detailed information on the binding mode of the AIE-Cdots (Fig. 1 g). The deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum contains three peaks at 284.7, 286.4, and 288.9 eV corresponding to C = C, C–N/C–O, and C = O, respectively (Fig. 1 h). The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 1 i indicates the presence of graphitic N (401.8 eV) and pyridine-N (399.2 eV) structures in the AIE-Cdots. The deconvoluted O 1 s peaks at 531.7 and 533.0 eV were attributed to C = O and C–O–H/C–O–C bonds, respectively. The XPS analysis results are consistent with those of the FT-IR analysis. These results demonstrate the successful formation of AIE-Cdots with abundant surface functional groups, such as –OH, –NH2, and –C = O.

Previous studies have investigated the effects of the polarity and viscosity of dispersion solvents on the self-assembly patterns of AIE-Cdots through spectroscopic techniques [53,54]. We also investigated the spectroscopic properties from the aggregation behavior of AIE-Cdots observed in different solvent environments for the proof of concept for AIE-Cdots designs (Fig. 2
a). AIE-Cdots were poorly soluble in acetonitrile (ACN), which is an aprotic solvent and was selected to observe a clear aggregation behavior. As evident from the absorption spectra in Fig. 2 b, the AIE-Cdots exhibited a noticeable solvent-dependent blue shift and increased absorbance (hyperchromic effect) in the 260–300 nm region, along with a polarity change of the dispersed solvent due to the increase in the ACN volume fraction. Such high-energy absorption bands appear only in aprotic solvents (ACN) with low hydrogen-bond donor index, which can be assigned to H-aggregates formed by hydrogen bonding between functional groups on the surface of AIE-Cdots without the disruption of H-aggregates by water [54–56].

Upon excitation at 390 nm, water-dispersed AIE-Cdots emitted weak fluorescence (λmax EM = 480 nm), while their self-aggregates in poor solvent (ACN) exhibited intense blue fluorescence at emission wavelengths similar to those shown in Fig. 2 c. TEM image measured under signal-amplified conditions revealed self-aggregates formed by AIE-Cdots (Fig. 2 d). These results suggest that the rotational functional group of AIE-Cdots enhanced emission while preventing non-radiative decay pathways by minimizing direct contact of particles, unlike the aggregation-caused quenching phenomenon occurring in common Cdots when the particles become closely packed. Based on the above results, we hypothesized that AIE features shown on the Cdots depended on the ribityl group, a side chain of the riboflavin used as a precursor. To verify this, other Cdots (Lumi-Cdots) were prepared using lumichrome dye with a backbone similar to riboflavin but without a ribityl side chain ( Fig. S4 ). Under the same environments (ACN/H2O = 99:1 v/v) where aggregation of Cdots was observed, Lumi-Cdots emitted weaker fluorescence than in the dispersed state while showing the blue-shifted emission changes. This result suggests that the ribityl side chain attached to riboflavin plays a crucial role in producing Cdots with AIE features ( Fig. S5 ).

The AIE effect of Cdots was confirmed in the presence of Al3+. Fig. 3
a shows the fluorescence spectrum of AIE-Cdots in HEPES buffer (pH 5.5) containing various metal ions (50 μM). The blue fluorescence of AIE-Cdots was amplified by the addition of Al3+, whereas no remarkable signal change was observed for other metal ions. The absorption band of AIE-Cdots with a hyperchromic effect was observed, along with a distinct color change (from light yellow to deep yellow) of the solution ( Fig. S6 ). The formation of nanoaggregates induced by Al3+ can be one of the reasons for the fluorescence enhancement because Cdots have an AIE effect. To clarify this, we analyzed the aggregation phenomenon using TEM, and the corresponding TEM images showing the nanoaggregates of AIE-Cdots generated by the addition of Al3+ are presented in Fig. 3 b. Furthermore, the EDX mapping images (Fig. 3 c) demonstrate the existence of Al3+, C, O, and N evenly distributed on Al3+-NAs, indicating that Al3+ mediates aggregation of AIE-Cdots. Along with a noticeable change in surface charge after adding Al3+ (Fig. 3 d), the DLS size distribution was shown to form micron-size aggregates ( Fig. S7 ).

The aggregation mechanism of AIE-Cdots upon the addition of Al3+ was demonstrated by the FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 e, a peak corresponding to the O coordination with metal ions at 1645 cm−1 more clearly appeared after adding Al3+. Two new peaks estimated as Al–O bonds were observed at 1231 and 1161 cm−1, indicating the formation of coordination bonds between the O of the carbonyl group and the Al3+ ion. The characteristic vibration peaks of –OH (∼3280–3500, and 1376 cm−1) in Al3+-NAs are weakened compared to that of the AIE-Cdots, which is attributable to be due to the strong H-bonding interaction between –OH groups on the AIE-Cdots surface by the Al3+-induced aggregation [57–59]. These results suggest that the aggregation behavior of AIE-Cdots is due to a favorable interaction between Al3+ (hard acid) and O (hard base) based on the hard-soft base-acid principle, as well as the strong H-bonding between –OH groups on the AIE-Cdots surface (Fig. 3 f).

The fluorescence response allows it to be completed within 2 min, and a stable signal was emitted for 30 min, confirming the good stability of Al3+-NAs (Fig. 4
a). The selectivity of AIE-Cdots was further investigated using competitive assays in binary mixture systems where other interfering metal ions were also present. The co-existence of Al3+ and other metal ions in the mixture slightly improved the fluorescence signal of AIE-Cdots; however, the fluorescence enhancement by Al3+ was evident (Fig. 4 b). As shown in Fig. 4 c and 4d, AIE-Cdots produced a sensitive fluorescence signal only for Al3+, exhibiting a linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) between the fluorescence and Al3+ in the concentration range of 0–100 μM with a detection limit of 0.89 μM. This result proves that AIE-Cdots have reasonable selectivity and sensitivity toward Al3+.

Such fluorescence enhancement occurs because of the Al3+–TC complex formed by the structural fixation of TC to Al3+ sites [60,61]. We found that the fluorescence of a solution containing only 100 μM Al3+ gradually increased with increasing concentrations of TC ( Fig. S11
a). In contrast, the fluorescence of the Al3+-NAs measured in the same TC concentration range (0, 1, 2, 5, 8, 10 μM) was significantly stronger than that of the Al3+–TC solution ( Fig. S11
b). Along with fluorescence enhancement by TC binding, the absorption peak of Al3+-NAs was red-shifted, which means that the conformational planarization of TC bound to the Al3+ site of Al3+-NAs ( Fig. S12 ). These results suggest the formation of nanoaggregates by coordinating TC to Al3+ sites without a fluorescence decrease accompanied by the disaggregation of Al3+-NAs. The TEM results in Fig. 5 f further support the presence of similar nanoaggregates identified in Al3+-NAs. The high-magnification image of an edge region of TC-Al3+-NAs shows that AIE-Cdots with the lattice fringes are firmly assembled inside the TC-Al3+-NAs (Fig. 5 g). The EDX mapping images are displayed in Fig. 5 h, from which it can be confirmed that all elements observed in Al3+-NAs are also well distributed on the TC-Al3+-NAs. In addition, there was no noticeable change in the FT-IR spectrum, including the characteristic peaks of C = O and Al–O bonds depending on the presence or absence of TC ( Fig. S13 ). These results support the formation of TC-Al3+-NAs by selective binding of TC to Al3+ active sites, suggesting the potential for utilization of Al3+-NAs for TC removal from wastewater. As shown in Table S1 , our nanoaggregate probe showed a distinct advantage in TC detection over the previously reported “turn-off” Cdots sensor, with a relatively low detection limit, rapid response, and selective TC adsorption ability.

The need to develop new types of antibiotics, such as metalloantibiotics, to treat bacterial infection has recently been highlighted [13,62,63]. Although yet to be fully explored to convert various antibiotics into metalloantibiotics, the superior coordination ability of TC has sufficient potential for development into metalloantibiotics. Given several nanostructures that have developed based on a robust binding interaction of TC to Al3+, Al3+–TC complexes may be formed when Al3+ and TC coexist. Considering the Al3+-mediated aggregation behavior of AIE-Cdots shown in the above results, we evaluated whether AIE-Cdots form similar nanoaggregates by Al3+–TC complexes.

For this, we compared the fluorescence signals produced from three samples (Batch 1, 2, and 3) prepared under different mixing conditions (Fig. 6
a). Batches 1 and 2 indicate that Al3+–TC complexes were added to an AIE-Cdot solution, or TC was added to a solution containing Al3+-NAs. Batch 3 refers to a solution in which Al3+ was added to a mixture containing AIE-Cdots and TC. As shown in Fig. 6 b, all batches showed very similar signals at emission wavelengths ranging from 440 to 650 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence signal of the AIE-Cdots–TC mixture was the same as that of AIE-Cdots, confirming that the fluorescence enhancement by the aggregation of AIE-Cdots was induced only by Al3+. The TEM (Fig. 6 c) and HR-TEM images (Fig. 6 d) of Batch 2 where Al3+ and TC were mixed preferentially also demonstrated the formation of nanoaggregates shown in Batch 1. These results suggest that AIE-Cdots form nanoaggregates with Al3+–TC complexes, and this aggregation behavior produces a strong fluorescence response in the nanoaggregates. Therefore, AIE-Cdots selectively mediated by Al3+ supports the feasibility of AIE-Cdots-based nanoplatforms for follow-up studies such as aluminum complexes monitoring.

In summary, we reported nanoaggregate probes based on AIE-Cdots with rotational functional groups for the detection of TC residues and Al3+–TC complexes. Solvent polarity-dependent spectroscopic studies revealed that AIE-Cdots could form H-type aggregates owing to the hydrogen bonding interactions of the surface side groups. Such aggregation behavior of AIE-Cdots was observed by the selective binding of Al3+, which led to fluorescence enhancement. Al3+-NAs with abundant Al3+ active sites achieved high selectivity and sensitivity for TC detection with further amplified signals, forming TC-coordinated nanoaggregates without disaggregation of AIE-Cdots. Furthermore, the strong Al3+ binding affinity of AIE-Cdots was successfully used to detect Al3+–TC complexes. The developed AIE-Cdots demonstrated their potential as turn-on nanoaggregate probes for monitoring Al3+–TC complexes and TC. Therefore, AIE-Cdots are expected to be applied to broadening applications such as environmental and food safety monitoring, disease theranostics, and forensic science.

Young Hun Seo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Diana Elizabeth Aguilar Estrada: . Dohyub Jang: . Seungyun Baik: . Jaeho Lee: . Dong Ha Kim: Supervision. Sehoon Kim: Funding acquisition, Supervision.
